## Background

The global cancer burden in older adults is expanding rapidly, yet oncology professionals' preparedness to address the long-term care (LTC) needs of elderly cancer patients remains poorly characterized. Elderly patients with cancer present unique challenges—including functional decline, multimorbidity, and competing non-oncologic care demands—that require deliberate referral pathways to geriatric and community-based LTC services [@Yamamoto2026; @Mashiro2023]. Despite growing recognition that survivorship extends well beyond treatment completion, systematic evidence on oncology professionals' knowledge deficits, attitudinal barriers, and referral behavior toward LTC for this population has not been synthesized.

## Methods

We conducted a systematic review in accordance with the PRISMA 2020 guidelines, searching three databases—Scopus, PubMed (MEDLINE), and Embase—from inception through March 2026 with no date restrictions. Search queries combined controlled vocabulary (MeSH: Neoplasms; Long-Term Care; Health Knowledge, Attitudes, Practice; Attitude of Health Personnel; Referral and Consultation) with free-text keywords targeting oncology professionals' knowledge, attitudes, and referral practices related to long-term care for elderly cancer patients. Eligible studies were original research (quantitative, qualitative, or mixed-methods) published in English in Q1/Q2 journals (CiteScore ≥ 3.0). Of 221 records identified, 20 duplicates were removed, 22 were excluded for low journal quality (CiteScore < 3.0), and 1 was excluded for an invalid DOI, yielding 173 records for full-text assessment. After excluding 93 records for insufficient relevance, 80 articles were included in the final synthesis. Quality appraisal was performed using the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) critical appraisal tools matched to each study design. Data were extracted on professional knowledge levels, attitudinal orientations, and referral frequency or intention, and synthesized narratively given heterogeneity in outcome measurement.

## Results

Across included studies, oncology professionals demonstrated consistent knowledge gaps regarding the long-term and late effects of cancer treatment in older patients, with insufficient specialist training identified as a primary driver of suboptimal referral [@BlanchHartigan2014; @Zebrack2004]. In a nationally representative U.S. survey of 1,130 oncologists and 1,020 primary care physicians, fewer than 5% of oncologists provided written survivorship care plans, and only 32% discussed who survivors should contact for ongoing follow-up [@BlanchHartigan2014]. Qualitative evidence from Japanese healthcare and LTC providers identified seven discrete barriers to medical collaboration, including inadequate information sharing on life perspectives and delayed disclosure of patients' end-of-life values [@Mashiro2023]. Geriatric assessment—an established tool for identifying LTC needs in older cancer patients—remained underutilized in most health systems due to workforce limitations and structural barriers, despite randomized evidence linking it to improved treatment toxicity and quality of life [@Yamamoto2026]. Referral behavior was strongly associated with clinician training: oncologists with dedicated survivorship education were twice as likely to initiate survivorship care discussions (OR 2.02; 95% CI 1.51–2.70) [@BlanchHartigan2014]. Transfer of follow-up to community or primary care providers was feasible in only 43% of assessed early-stage breast cancer patients, with clinical trial enrollment and ongoing treatment cited as the primary obstacles [@Vanhuyse2007].

## Conclusion

Oncology professionals across health systems harbor substantive knowledge gaps and attitudinal reservations toward LTC referral for elderly cancer patients, with systemic barriers compounding individual-level deficits. Training-focused interventions and structured interorganizational protocols linking designated cancer centers to community LTC networks represent the most evidence-supported strategies for improving referral behavior. Future research should prioritize implementation trials in geriatric oncology settings and develop standardized metrics for measuring referral competency.
